1: YHWH ḥaqartanî watēdāʿ — "O LORD, you have searched me and you know me" — ḥāqar (H2713, to probe, examine thoroughly, penetrate) — from its basic meaning of digging through rock to examine ore, extended to intensive investigation; in the legal sphere, cross-examination; here applied to divine scrutiny of the inner person. The Qal perfect ḥaqartanî (you have searched me) presents the act as already accomplished: the search is complete and ongoing in its result. watēdāʿ (and you know) — the result of the search is full knowledge; not knowledge despite gaps, but comprehensive knowledge. This opening verse establishes the psalm's central theme: divine omniscience directed personally and intimately at the psalmist.
2: ʾattāh yādaʿtā šibtî wĕqûmî bantāh lĕrēʿî mērāḥôq — "you know when I sit and when I stand; you understand my thoughts before I even form them" — šibtî wĕqûmî (my sitting and my rising) — a merism for all postures and activities; the two extremes of physical rest and motion encompass everything between. bantāh lĕrēʿî mērāḥôq — "you discern my thought from afar" — rēaʿ (thought, purpose, intention). mērāḥôq (from afar, from a distance) — before the thought is articulated or even fully formed, YHWH already perceives it from his transcendent vantage. The paradox of omniscient intimacy: God sees the innermost intention from a divine distance.
3: ʾorḥî wĕrĕbî zērîtā wĕkol-dĕrākaî hiSkantāh — "you trace every path I walk and every place I rest; you are familiar with all my ways" — ʾōraḥ (path, way traveled) and rebaʿ (lying down, resting place) — the merism of movement and rest. zērîtā (you winnow/sift out, you measure) — from zārāh (to scatter, winnow, sift); YHWH sifts through the psalmist's paths as a farmer sifts grain — nothing passes unexamined. hiSkantāh (you are intimately familiar with) — from sākan (to be familiar with, accustomed to); YHWH has made himself thoroughly at home in the psalmist's ways.
4: kî ʾên millāh bilĕšônî hēn YHWH yādaʿtā kullāh — "before a word is on my tongue, you know it completely, O LORD" — ʾên millāh (there is no word) — the word does not yet exist on the tongue; it is still unspoken. hēn YHWH yādaʿtā kullāh (behold, O LORD, you know it all/completely) — hēn (behold) signals the astonishing character of what follows; kullāh (all of it, the whole of it) is emphatic. YHWH knows the word before it has become a word at all — while it is still an intention seeking articulation.
5: ʾāḥôr wāqedem ṣartanî watāšet ʿālay kappekā — "you surround me on every side — behind and before — and you have laid your hand upon me" — ʾāḥôr wāqedem (behind and before/east and west) — another merism for totality; in Hebrew orientation, qedem is east (the direction faced), so before + behind = east + west = all directions. ṣartanî (you have besieged me, you have pressed/enclosed me) — from ṣûr (to press, besiege, lay siege); the metaphor is military encirclement. watāšet ʿālay kappekā (you have laid your palm upon me) — kap (palm, hollow of the hand) — the resting of the divine palm: a gesture of both protection and constraint. The omnipresent God who encircles also touches.
6: palʾiyyāh daʿat mimmennî niśgĕbāh lōʾ-ʾûkal lāh — "this knowledge is beyond me — too wonderful, too high for me to grasp" — palʾiyyāh (it is too wonderful, miraculous, Niphal participle of pālāʾ) — the root of niplāʾôt (wonders, miracles); the divine knowledge is classified in the same category as the Exodus miracles — beyond human capacity to achieve or comprehend. niśgĕbāh (it is too high, too lofty, Niphal of śāgab) — the knowledge is not merely intellectually difficult but ontologically beyond reach. The response to omniscience is not argument but awe.
7: ʾānāh ʾēlēk mērûḥekā wĕʾānāh mippānêkā ʾebraḥ — "where can I go to escape your Spirit? Where can I flee from your presence?" — rûaḥekā (your Spirit, your breath) — the divine rûaḥ that fills all space; the omnipresence of YHWH expressed through his Spirit. pānêkā (your face, your presence) — the "face" of God is the expression of his active personal presence; to flee from the face is to attempt escape from the immediate divine attention (cf. Jonah 1:3, 10, using the same idiom). The rhetorical questions have no answer — the omnipresence is total. But the psalm's tone is wonder, not threat: the impossibility of escape is simultaneously the impossibility of abandonment.
8: ʾim-ʾessaq šāmayim šām ʾattāh wĕʾaṣṣîʿāh šĕʾôl hinnekā — "if I rise to heaven, you are there; if I make my bed in the grave, you are there too" — šāmayim (heavens) — the highest accessible realm. šĕʾôl (Sheol) — the place of the dead, the underworld; in Hebrew cosmology the antipode of heaven. The merism heaven/Sheol encompasses the full vertical extent of the cosmos. hinnekā (there you are, behold you) — in both extremes YHWH is present. The theological significance: Sheol in the Hebrew Bible was sometimes imagined as beyond YHWH's reach (Psalms 6:5; Psalms 88:5); Ps 139 corrects this — even the realm of the dead is not a zone of divine absence.
9: ʾessāʾ kanpê-šāḥar ʾeškĕnāh bĕʾaḥărît hayyām — "if I fly away on the wings of the dawn and settle at the farthest edge of the sea" — kanpê-šāḥar (wings of the dawn/morning) — šāḥar (dawn, morning-light); the "wings" of the dawn are the spreading rays of light at sunrise, sweeping from east to west. The psalmist imagines riding this light to the farthest western horizon. bĕʾaḥărît hayyām (at the farthest end of the sea) — the western Mediterranean was the far horizon of the ancient Israelite world. The horizontal axis (east to west, dawn to sea's end) completes the directional coverage with the vertical axis (heaven to Sheol, v8) — omnipresence in all dimensions.
10: gam-šām yādĕkā tanḥēnî wĕtōʾḥăzēnî yĕmînĕkā — "even there your hand will guide me, and your right hand will hold me fast" — tanḥēnî (Hiphil of nāḥāh, to guide, lead) — the same verb used for the divine guidance through the wilderness (Exodus 15:13). tōʾḥăzēnî yĕmînĕkā (your right hand will hold me fast) — ʾāḥaz (to seize, grasp, hold); the "holding" is firm, not passive. The two hands — the guiding hand and the holding right hand — are the hands of shepherd and warrior simultaneously: the omnipresent God guides and secures.
11: waʾōmar ʾak-ḥōšek yĕšûpēnî wĕlaylāh ʾôr baʿădênî — "if I say, 'The darkness will surely hide me, and the light around me turn to night'" — ʾak-ḥōšek yĕšûpēnî (surely the darkness will cover/overwhelm me) — šûp is a rare verb; in Genesis 3:15 it appears in the serpent/seed conflict ("he will strike/crush your head"). Here it may mean "overwhelm, engulf": the darkness will surely swallow me up. The psalmist voices the hypothetical attempt to use darkness as concealment from the divine presence — immediately refuted in v12.
12: gam-ḥōšek lōʾ-yaḥšîk mimmekā wĕlaylāh kayyôm yāʾîr kĕḥōšekāh kāʾôrāh — "even the darkness is not dark to you; the night shines like the day, for darkness and light are the same to you" — lōʾ-yaḥšîk mimmekā (it is not dark from you, it does not grow dark before you) — ḥāšak (to be dark); the divine perception is not subject to the light spectrum available to humans. kayyôm yāʾîr (the night lights up like the day) — before YHWH's perceiving presence, the night is as transparent as day. kĕḥōšekāh kāʾôrāh (darkness as light to you) — the definitive statement of divine omniscience through the lens of light: no concealment is possible.
13: kî-ʾattāh qānîtā kilyōtāy tĕsukēkēnî bĕbeṭen ʾimmî — "for you formed my inward parts; you wove me together in my mother's womb" — qānîtā kilyōtāy (you formed/acquired my kidneys) — qānāh (H7069, to create, erect, acquire/possess) — used of God's creating in Genesis 14:19, 22 ("creator of heaven and earth") and Proverbs 8:22 ("the LORD possessed/created me"). kilyôt (kidneys, H3629) — the kidneys in Hebrew anthropology are the seat of the deepest emotions and moral sensibilities (Psalms 7:9; Psalms 16:7; Psalms 26:2; Jeremiah 11:20; Psalms 17:10) — what we would call the "gut" or conscience. YHWH formed the very organ of moral feeling. tĕsukēkēnî (you wove me together, covered me) — from sāḵaḵ (to weave, intertwine, cover as with a curtain); the body's intricate tissue is imaged as the embroiderer's skilled work. This verse provides the theological foundation for prenatal personhood in the psalm.
14: ʾôdĕkā ʿal kî nôrāʾôt niplêtî niplāʾîm maʿăśêkā wĕnapšî yōdaʿat mĕʾōd — "I praise you because I am fearfully and wonderfully made — your works are marvellous, and my whole being knows it" — nôrāʾôt (fearfully, in an awe-inspiring manner) and niplāʾîm (wonderfully, in a wonder-producing manner) — both participial forms expressing the quality of the creation act. niplêtî (I am wonderfully made, Niphal perfect of pālāʾ) — the psalmist is the passive recipient of the wonderful making; the wonder belongs to the act, not the psalmist's subjective feeling. wĕnapšî yōdaʿat mĕʾōd (and my soul/whole being knows it very well) — the recognition of the wondrous making is located in the nepeš (the whole living self), not merely the intellect.
15: lōʾ-nikad ʿaṣmî mimmekā ʾăšer-ʿuśśîtî basSēter rōqamtî bĕtaḥtiyyôt ʾāreṣ — "my very frame was not hidden from you when I was made in secret, skillfully crafted in the depths of the earth" — ʿaṣmî (my bones, my body-frame) — ʿeṣem can mean bone (structure) or the bodily self; here the physical structure in its making. basSēter (in the secret/hidden place) — the womb as a hidden place of formation; the "secret" is not concealment from God but the hidden depths of creaturely beginning. rōqamtî (I was skillfully crafted, embroidered, wrought with varied colors) — from rāqam (to embroider, weave in colored patterns, used of tabernacle textiles in Exodus 26:36; Psalms 35:35); the body's intricate tissue and varied organ-structure is imaged as the embroiderer's skilled work. bĕtaḥtiyyôt ʾāreṣ (in the depths of the earth) — a poetic parallel to "in the womb"; the womb as the lowest hidden place of earth-bound existence.
16: gālemî rāʾû ʿênêkā wĕʿal-siprekā kullām yikkātēbû yāmîm yuṣṣārû ûlō ʾeḥād bāhem — "your eyes saw my unformed body; all my days were written in your book before any of them had come to be" — gōlemî (my unformed mass, my embryo) — H1564, a hapax legomenon appearing only here in the entire Hebrew Bible. The root gālam means to wrap up; the gōlem is the wrapped, unformed embryonic mass before it has differentiated into recognizable form. YHWH saw the pre-formed embryo before it was recognizable as a person. sēper (book, scroll) — the divine record in which the psalmist's days are inscribed before they exist; a book of providential knowledge, not fatalistic predetermination but the comprehensive foreknowledge of the God who "completes" all things (Psalms 138:8). kullām yikkātēbû (all of them were written) — the totality of days, written before any came into existence.
17: ûlî mah-yāqĕrû rēʿêkā ʾēl mah ʿāṣĕmû rāʾšêhem — "how priceless are your thoughts toward me, O God! How immeasurable their total!" — mah-yāqĕrû (how precious/rare they are) — yāqar (to be precious, costly, honored; rare): the divine thoughts directed toward the psalmist are both precious and beyond ordinary occurrence. rēʿêkā (your thoughts, your plans, your purposes) — the plural of rēaʿ (thought, purpose, the directed cognitive act). mah ʿāṣĕmû rāʾšêhem (how vast their sum!) — an attempt to count the thoughts toward the psalmist would produce a sum too large to compute.
18: ʾespĕrēm mēḥôl yirbûn hĕqîṣōtî wĕʿôdî ʿimmāk — "if I tried to count them, they would be more than the grains of sand; and when I wake, I am still with you" — mēḥôl yirbûn (they are more than sand) — the classic biblical hyperbole for uncountable number (Genesis 22:17; Psalms 32:12; Isaiah 48:19). hĕqîṣōtî wĕʿôdî ʿimmāk (when I wake, I am still with you) — the stunning ending to the meditation on omnipresence: even after sleeping and waking, after all the contemplation has ended, the psalmist finds themselves still in the divine presence. The waking is not a new arrival into God's presence — it is the continuation of a presence that was never interrupted.
19: ʾim-tiqṭōl ʾĕlōhîm rāšāʿ wĕʾanšê dāmîm sûrû mimmennî — "O God, if only you would put an end to the wicked! Get away from me, you murderers!" — the psalm pivots dramatically from meditation on omniscience (vv1–18) to imprecation against the wicked (vv19–22) before closing with self-examination (vv23–24). tiqṭōl rāšāʿ (slay the wicked) — the jussive prayer that God would remove the wicked; not the psalmist's act but God's. ʾanšê dāmîm (men of blood, murderers) — a technical term for violent offenders throughout the Psalter (Psalms 5:6; Psalms 26:9; Psalms 55:23; Psalms 59:2; 2 Samuel 16:7). The pivot is not arbitrary: the God who knows the psalmist perfectly (vv1–18) also knows the wicked perfectly — and the psalmist appeals to that omniscience for justice.
20: ʾăšer yōmĕrûkā limzimmāh nāśūʾ laššāwĕʾ ʿārêkā — "they speak against you with evil intent; your adversaries invoke your name for nothing" — limzimmāh (for evil intent, for wicked scheme) — from zimmāh (devise, scheme, wickedness); the wicked's speech is not casual irreverence but deliberate plotting. lāššāwĕʾ (for falsehood, emptiness, vanity — the same šāwĕʾ of the third commandment: "do not take the name of the LORD in vain") — the wicked invoke YHWH's name for empty, false, or manipulative purposes.
21: hălōʾ-mĕśanʾêkā YHWH ʾeśnāʾ ûbitqôměmêkā ʾetqôṭāṭ — "LORD, don't I hate those who hate you? Don't I despise those who rise up against you?" — mĕśanʾêkā (those who hate you) — śānēʾ (to hate, despise). The sentiment is not personal hatred but covenantal solidarity: to align with God is to align against what opposes him. This is the language of covenant loyalty, paralleling the Deuteronomic call to love what God loves and oppose what God opposes (Deuteronomy 7:9–10).
22: tašlît śinʾāh śĕnēʾtîm lĕʾôyĕbîm hāyû lî — "I hate them with an absolute hatred; I number them among my enemies" — tašlît śinʾāh śĕnēʾtîm (with a complete/total hatred I hate them) — tašlît from šālam (to be whole, complete); the hatred is whole and undivided, without partial loyalty. lĕʾôyĕbîm hāyû lî (they have become enemies to me) — those who are enemies to YHWH are, by covenant solidarity, enemies of the psalmist. This verse is theologically difficult for Christian readers, who are called to love enemies (Matthew 5:44). The psalm speaks within the OT covenantal framework; the NT transforms this framework by specifying a new way of dealing with personal enemies — without canceling the reality that opposition to God is serious.
23: ḥāqĕrēnî ʾēl wĕdaʿ lĕbābî bĕḥānēnî ûdaʿ śarʿappāy — "search me, O God, and know my heart; put me to the test and see what I am thinking" — the prayer of vv23–24 invites upon the psalmist the very omniscient scrutiny that vv1–6 described as inevitable. ḥāqĕrēnî (search me, imperative of ḥāqar) — the same verb as v1; the psalmist actively summons the divine searching that was already described as ongoing. This is not confidence in personal righteousness but confidence that the God who sees completely will judge truly. bĕḥānēnî (test/try me, refine me) — from bāḥan (to test, refine as metals are refined); YHWH is invited to apply the refiner's examination. śarʿappāy (my anxious thoughts, my disquieting thoughts, pl. of śarʿap) — the divided, branching thoughts that cause inner conflict and anxiety; the psalmist asks God to see even these.
24: ûrĕʾēh ʾim-derek-ʿōṣeb bî ûnĕḥēnî bĕderek ʿôlām — "see if there is any harmful path in me, and lead me in the way that lasts forever" — derek-ʿōṣeb (a path of pain/sorrow, a grievous way) — ʿōṣeb (pain, sorrow, hurt) — a path characterized by anguish; the root may also connect to ʿeṣeb (idol), so the phrase could carry the sense of idolatrous ways. The psalmist does not ask to be declared innocent but asks God to identify any hidden harmful way — a posture of honest openness to divine judgment. derek ʿôlām (the way that lasts forever, the everlasting way) — ʿôlām (eternity, forever, of old); this may mean the ancient tried way (the covenant path of the ancestors) or the way that leads to eternal life — the two meanings coexist. The psalm that opened with divine searching closes with the human invitation of that searching — a movement from received omniscience to requested examination.
